coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and l-cysteine by means of fluorescence “Off–On” switching
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Shi, Dechao
Zheng, Tancheng
Yun, Kaiyi
Zhou, Xuguang
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: An “Off–On” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) and l-cysteine (l-Cys) was demonstrated with the use of water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) as novel fluorescent probe. In this present sensing system, Hg2+ cations can efficiently coordinate onto the surface of CDs by means of electrostatic interactions and remarkably quench the fluorescence signal of CDs as a result of the fluorescence charge transfer (turn-off). In addition, the resulting CDs-Hg2+ system was observed to be sensitive to l-Cys due to the ability of l-Cys to coordinate with Hg2+ and the formation of strong Hg2+-SR bond. When l-Cys was added, Hg2+ combined with l-Cys rather than with CDs resulting in the remove of Hg2+ from the surface of CDs and shielding of fluorescence quenching, and thus, a significant fluorescence restore of CDs was observed (turn-on). Under the optimized conditions, good linearity for detecting Hg2+ was attained over the concentration range from 2 to 22μM with a detection limit of 0.017μM, while the concentrations of l-Cys ranging from 2 to 20μM exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.99329. Moreover, this CDs-based sensor was successfully applied for visual identification and quantitation of Hg2+ and l-Cys in real water sample and fluorescence imaging of NIH-3T3 cells with satisfying resolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Mercury ions
l-Cysteine
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and l-Cys
Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
The feasibility of the CDs for Hg2+ detection
Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
Mechanism of the detection of l-Cys
Sensitivity of the novel sensor for l-Cys
Specificity of the CDs-Hg2+ system for l-Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra for the CDs. (b) PL emission spectra of CDs with variations of excitation wavelength.
(a) Typical TEM image of CDs (inset: the diameter distribution of CDs), (b) FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CDs, (c) XPS survey scan, (d) C1s, (e) N1s, (f) O1s XPS spectrum of CDs.

(a) Selective detection of Hg2+ using CDs as probe (Hg2+ concentration: 10μM; Metal ions concentration: 100μM). Inset: Bar chart: responses of the CDs obtained in the present of Hg2+ and other metal ions. Excitation at 350nm. (b) Photographs of the aqueous CDs containing various metal ions under UV light (excited at 365nm).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentrations. (b) Plots of the values of F 1/F 0 at 430nm versus the concentration of Hg2+. Inset: a linear relationship between (F 1/F 0)−1 and Hg2+ concentration from 2 to 22μM with a correlation coefficient R 2 =0.99284. Excitation at 350nm.
UV–vis absorption spectra of: (a) CDs; (b) CDs+Hg2+; and (c) CDs+Hg2+ + l-Cys.

(a) Fluorescence response of CDs-Hg2+ in the presence of increasing concentration of l-Cys (from 0 to 20μM). λ ex =350nm. (b) The picture shows a linear relationship over the concentration of l-Cys in the range from 2 to 20μM. Excitation at 350nm.

(a) Selectivity of the CDs-Hg2+ system toward l-Cys. The concentration of l-Cys and other amino acids were both 10μM. Inset: Corresponding line chart of the responses of CDs-Hg2+ system obtained in the present of various amino acids. Excitation at 350nm. (b) Photos of CDs-Hg2+ system by UV irradiation at 365nm in the presence of various amino acids. (c) Interference study of using CDs-Hg2+ as sensor for l-Cys premixed with other amino acids (100μM). Excitation at 350nm.

(A) Cell viability of NIH-3T3 and HL-7702 cells treated with various concentrations of CDs as estimated by MTT assay. (B) (a) Bright-field transmission image, and (b) fluorescence image of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with 0.01mgmL−1 CDs for 24h at 37°C; (c) Bright-field transmission image, and (d) fluorescence image of NIH-3T3 cells which were first incubated with 0.01mgmL−1 CDs for 24h and then incubated with 10μM Hg2+ for about ∼30min at 37°C; (e) Bright-field transmission image, and (f) fluorescence image of NIH-3T3 cells which were first incubated with 0.01mgmL−1 CDs for 24h and 10μM Hg2+ for 30min and then incubated with 10μM l-Cys for ∼30min at 37°C.
The detecting pathway for Hg2+ and l-Cys based on the fluorescence switching of CDs.
Performance comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The solvent is water, pH maintained at neutral.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and l-cysteine by means of fluorescence “Off–On” switching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: TianJin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: TianJin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An “Off–On” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) and l-cysteine (l-Cys) was demonstrated with the use of water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) as novel fluorescent probe. In this present sensing system, Hg2+ cations can efficiently coordinate onto the surface of CDs by means of electrostatic interactions and remarkably quench the fluorescence signal of CDs as a result of the fluorescence charge transfer (turn-off). In addition, the resulting CDs-Hg2+ system was observed to be sensitive to l-Cys due to the ability of l-Cys to coordinate with Hg2+ and the formation of strong Hg2+-SR bond. When l-Cys was added, Hg2+ combined with l-Cys rather than with CDs resulting in the remove of Hg2+ from the surface of CDs and shielding of fluorescence quenching, and thus, a significant fluorescence restore of CDs was observed (turn-on). Under the optimized conditions, good linearity for detecting Hg2+ was attained over the concentration range from 2 to 22μM with a detection limit of 0.017μM, while the concentrations of l-Cys ranging from 2 to 20μM exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.99329. Moreover, this CDs-based sensor was successfully applied for visual identification and quantitation of Hg2+ and l-Cys in real water sample and fluorescence imaging of NIH-3T3 cells with satisfying resolution.

ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of adverse effects in many biological processes, such as bone, brain, kidney, liver, immune system, central nervous system and cardiovascular, have been attributed to mercury ions (Hg2+) poisoning [1–5]. Thus, it is considered as one of the most hazardous and toxic cations on human health [6–9]. Up to 3000tons of anthropogenic mercury have been released into the atmosphere yearly [10]. Due to its non-biodegradation, the absorbed mercury can only be diluted or transformed and finally accumulate in organisms through the food chain, instead of be destroyed [11]. Its serious hazard can be confirmed by the well-known case of the Minamata disease discovered in Japan [12]. Therefore, it is necessary to underscore the need for analytical methods to facilitate the control of Hg2+. Up to now, a range of techniques and protocols, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [13,14], inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [15,16], capillary electrophoresis [17], have been well developed and utilized to monitor the level of Hg2+. However, these methods generally suffer from several limitations, e.g. they need expensive instrumentation, complicated operation procedures and time-consuming sample pretreatment, which greatly limit their practical application of the methods for rapid on-site analysis [18,19]. For routine analysis, the fluorescent methods have therefore received significant attention and been widely employed to detect targets in recent years because of their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, ease of operation, and the lack of necessity of a reference solution [20,21].

In recent years, various small organic [22,23], polygenic [24,25], and inorganic materials [26,27] have been developed to monitor and analyze Hg2+ by the fluorescence spectroscopic method. Most representatively, small molecule organic fluorescent dyes and nano-quantum dots (QDs) were used as sensitive analytical sensors for analysis of substances. Such sensors possess certain advantages, e.g. high sensitivity, short analysis time, low cost and simple treatment process. Unfortunately, the application of QDs is limited since the serious health and environmental concerns caused by heavy metals, while organic dyes suffer from low photo-stability. Therefore, it is highly demanded to develop new nanomaterials to apply as excellent selective and sensitive probes that can overcome the above limitations. Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous research interest due to their unique optical properties and promising biomedical applications [28–32]. Compared to the conventional heavy-metal-based QDs and organic fluorescent dyes, CDs have generated enormous excitements because of their superiority in water solubility, good biocompatibility, low-toxicity, low cost, high photo-stability and ease of functionalization [33–37]. In particular, based on their strong and tuneable photoluminescence and biocompatible properties, carbon dot-based nanosensors were successfully implemented in biological imaging and detection areas [38–42]. Nevertheless, significant research is still necessary to explore the full potential of these nanomaterials in the development of advanced smart sensors.

l-cysteine (l-Cys), an essential component of many proteins and organism, is involved in many important physiological processes. As a small-molecular-weight biothiol, l-Cys plays an important role in maintaining the biological redox homeostasis through the mutual transformation between free thiols and disulfides in vivo [43]. The detection and quantification of l-Cys can contribute to the diagnoses, monitoring, prevention and treatments of several diseases, such as skin lesions, liver damage, cardiovascular disease and Alzheimer's disease [44,45]. Therefore, developing relatively inexpensive, reliable and rapid methods for the analysis of l-Cys is highly desirable. Wei et al. developed a colorimetric method based on the aggregation of gold nanoparticles induced by l-Cys due to the substitute of carboxymethylcellulose by the thiol group [46]. The above aggregation will change the color of gold nanoparticles from purple to blue, and thus, a relationship between UV–vis absorbance and l-Cys concentration could be modeled. Li et al. exploited a simple method for Hg2+ and l-Cys analysis with the use of fluorescence switching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [47]. This occurred based on the fact that the Hg2+ induced aggregation of GQDs led to significant fluorescence quenching due to charge transfer, while the addition of l-Cys resulted in fluorescence restore of the GQDs because a strong metal-thiol bond formed. Zong et al. demonstrated an “OFF–ON” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys using CDs as fluorescent probe [44]. But they failed to describe any biological applications with the synthesized materials.

By taking into account all the above considerations, we reported a kind of CDs obtained from citric acid and biuret, as label-free fluorometric biosensor, are capable of rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+ and l-Cys in a wide concentration range. For present CDs, the detection of Hg2+ is based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence of CDs, while the detection of l-Cys is due to the binding preference of l-Cys toward Hg2+ through an Hg2+-SR bond. l-Cys interacts with Hg2+ preferentially than CDs and removes Hg2+ from the surface of CDs, and thus the florescence of the system could be restored. Good linearity for detecting Hg2+ was attained over the concentration range from 2 to 22μM with a detection limit of 0.017μM. Under the optimized conditions, the concentrations of l-Cys ranging from 2 to 20μM exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.99329. Moreover, this CDs-based sensor has been used for visual identification and quantitation of Hg2+ and l-Cys in real water sample and successfully applied for the fluorescence imaging of NIH-3T3 cells.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.5%) and biuret were purchased from the local company. Quinine sulfate (99%, suitable for fluorescence) was supplied by Aladdin. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye was purchased from Aladdin. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Double distilled water was used in all experiments.

The CDs were synthesized as follows: a transparent solution containing citric acid (3g), biuret (3g), and distilled water (100mL) was heated for 12min in a 600W microwave oven. After the solution changed to a dark-brown clustered solid, sodium hydroxide was added into the aqueous solution containing this solid to adjust the pH to 14. The solid obtained by rotary evaporation was dissolved in ethanol. After removing the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation, the final product was obtained by rotary evaporation.

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL JEM 2011 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The specimen was prepared by drop casting the sample dispersion onto a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at room temperature. The absorption spectra were measured on a UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation wavelength of 350nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer within the range of 400–4000cm−1. The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). The zeta-potential was measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of CDs were dissolved in 0.1molL−1 PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) to afford 0.01mgmL−1 aqueous solution. 100μL of the CDs solution were added to 10mL volumetric flasks, then Hg2+ solution (10−3 molL−1) ranging from 0 to 200μL were added and constant volume with PBS buffer solution. For comparison, 100μL of different metal ions (10−3 molL−1) were added to the volumetric flasks containing CDs solution. All samples were incubated for 30min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 350nm. For the detection of l-Cys, different amounts of l-Cys (10−3 M) were added into the mixture solution of CDs (100μL, 0.01mgmL−1) and Hg2+ (100μL, 10−3 molL−1). The final concentration of l-Cys ranged from 0 to 20μM. All samples were incubated for 30min at room temperature and recorded under excitation at 350nm to obtain the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
ce:section.ce:para: NIH-3T3 cells were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. One day before imaging, cells were place in 6-well flat-bottomed plates. The culture medium was replaced by 2.5mL fresh medium containing 0.01mgmL−1 CDs and the cells were incubated for another 24h. After being washed with fresh 1×PBS (pH 7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on the inverted fluorescence microscope. Then, the cells were exposed to 10μM Hg2+ for 30min. The cells were imaged after washing the culture medium three times with PBS. Finally, 10μM l-Cys was added and incubated for ∼30min at 37°C. The cells were imaged again after washing the culture medium three times with PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid is an important organic acid which is commonly being used in food industry and cosmetic production, while biuret was widely used as pharmaceutical intermediates abundant in nitrogen elements. In addition, microwave irradiation provides an easy way of synthesizing CDs within minutes [48]. Therefore, the successful preparation of such CDs has been carried out by microwave-assisted treatment of citric acid and biuret, which produced dark-brown clustered solid, indicating successful carbonization of raw materials. To explore the optical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra in aqueous solution were investigated and shown in Fig. 1(a). The typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs showed a peak at 330nm, owing to n–π* transition of CO and π–π* transition of CC. The CDs aqueous solution emitted blue fluorescence on exposure to a 365nm UV lamp while appearing as completely colorless transparent under daylight. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs with variations of excitation wavelength (290–420nm) were shown in Fig. 1(b). When the excitation wavelength was 350nm, a strong PL emission peak located at 430nm was observed. The emission peak was also shifted to a higher wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength, which can be explained by an “edge red-shift effect” [49]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was found to be 15.3% with quinine sulfate as a reference.

The HRTEM images were employed to directly observe the morphology and particle size distribution of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2(a), these CDs are uniform, well-dispersed and no aggregation emerged, with a relatively narrow particle size distribution ranging from 3.0 to 6.0nm and an average diameter of 5.0nm. More and more evidences indicate that the fluorescence of CDs can be affected by the surface groups and structure [50]. Thus, the surface state of these synthesized CDs was explored by FT-IR spectroscopy in detail. As revealed in Fig. 2(b), there existed NH, CH, CO, CC and CO groups on the surface of CDs. Specifically, the broad absorption bands at 3300–3500cm−1 are assigned to NH groups, which illustrates that the amino groups were located on the surface of CDs. The peaks at 2850 and 2922cm−1 are attributed to the CH stretching vibration, while the peak at 1309cm−1 is associated with CH and NH bending vibration. Simultaneously, the peak at 1667cm−1 correspond to the CC stretching vibration. A carbonyl group (CO) band at 1621cm−1, a CO peak at 1076cm−1, and an OH band at 3207cm−1 appeared indicating the presence of hydrophilic groups, which explains the good water-solubility of CDs. Moreover, the surface components of the current CDs were also investigated by XPS analysis. The XPS spectrum showed three peaks at 285.24, 400.26 and 531.97eV, which can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2(c)). The C1s spectrum showed three peaks at 284.8, 285.9 and 289.4eV (Fig. 2(d)), which are associated with CC, CN and CO bonding respectively. The N1s spectrum showed in Fig. 2(e) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at around 398.4, 400.1 and 401.6eV, which can be assigned to CNC, NC and NH groups, respectively. To fairly describe the peak of N, the amplified peak is shown in Fig. 2(f), two fitted peaks at 531.4 and 532.3eV are ascribed to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively.

Then, the effects of temperature, irradiation time, pH value, ionic strength on the fluorescence stability of CDs were evaluated. Fig. S1(a) showed the influence of different temperatures on the fluorescence intensity. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of CDs kept stable when the temperature increased from 10 to 50°C, which indicated that temperature had a little effect on the character of CDs. Then, the obtained CDs were irradiated by UV light (365nm) for 6h in air and results suggested that the fluorescence intensity had no significant change, which indicated that CDs possess excellent photo-stability (Fig. S1(b)). As shown in Fig. S1(c), when the pH of solution was changed from 2.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity increased firstly and subsequently decreased, and CDs showed the strongest fluorescence intensity when pH was 7.0. The results suggested that CDs was insensitive to pH near 7.0 and could work in physiological pH conditions. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was tested by adding different concentration of NaCl solutions (from 0.1 to 3.0molL−1) into CDs aqueous solution at room temperature. Fig. S1(d) indicated that the ionic strength has a relatively small effect on the fluorescence intensity with no clear trend even at a high concentration. All of above results revealed that the CDs possess high stability, which are profoundly suitable for fluorescent detection.

Then, the effects of temperature, irradiation time, pH value, ionic strength on the fluorescence stability of CDs were evaluated. Fig. S1(a) showed the influence of different temperatures on the fluorescence intensity. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of CDs kept stable when the temperature increased from 10 to 50°C, which indicated that temperature had a little effect on the character of CDs. Then, the obtained CDs were irradiated by UV light (365nm) for 6h in air and results suggested that the fluorescence intensity had no significant change, which indicated that CDs possess excellent photo-stability (Fig. S1(b)). As shown in Fig. S1(c), when the pH of solution was changed from 2.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity increased firstly and subsequently decreased, and CDs showed the strongest fluorescence intensity when pH was 7.0. The results suggested that CDs was insensitive to pH near 7.0 and could work in physiological pH conditions. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity was tested by adding different concentration of NaCl solutions (from 0.1 to 3.0molL−1) into CDs aqueous solution at room temperature. Fig. S1(d) indicated that the ionic strength has a relatively small effect on the fluorescence intensity with no clear trend even at a high concentration. All of above results revealed that the CDs possess high stability, which are profoundly suitable for fluorescent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the CDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility and specificity of using these CDs for Hg2+ detection, the fluorescence responses of CDs toward Hg2+ at a constant concentration were investigated in the present of various potentially interfering metal ions under the same condition. Fig. 3(a) indicated that even at 10 times higher concentrations than that of Hg2+, no apparent changes of fluorescence intensity were detected for 100-fold of other metal ions. The corresponding pictures of the color changes of the CDs (Fig. 3(b)), taken under a UV light (365nm), indicated the changes in the processes of the “On–Off”. Therefore, these potential interfering substances exhibited little influence on Hg2+ detection. These results clearly demonstrate that the proposed CDs possess outstanding selectivity for Hg2+ and can serve as novel fluorescence sensor for highly selective and reliable Hg2+ monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Some parameters, including incubation time, temperature and pH value, may have impacts on the detection system. To improve the performance of CDs sensor for Hg2+, we optimized the detection conditions before quantitative analysis of Hg2+ using the proposed method. At room temperature, the effect of the incubation time on the reaction system was shown in Fig. S2(a). It is clear that the quenched effect reached maximum when the reaction time was 30min and then sustained relatively stable. Hence, 30min was identified as the optimal incubation time. As revealed in Fig. S2(b), with the temperature increased from 10 to 60°C, the quenched effect of Hg2+ toward CDs kept stable, which suggested that temperature showed no effect on the detection character of CDs. To make sure obtain stable signal, the effect of pH on the reaction system was also tested. The result reveals that when the pH was changed from 3.0 to 9.0, the ration F 1/F 0 (F 0 and F 1 are fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively) increased firstly and then decreased, and the best quenched effect was achieved at 6–8 (Fig. S2(c)). At low pH, most of the functional groups are protonated and the competition of H+ with Hg(II) decreases the active sites. However, under alkaline conditions, the Hg2+ reacts with the OH− to form Hg(OH)2, which readily converts to HgO precipitate. Consequently, the quenched effect of the Hg2+ would be significantly weakened under acid or alkaline condition. So the 7.4 was chosen as the favorable pH due to pH 7.4 is similar to the physiological pH environment.

Some parameters, including incubation time, temperature and pH value, may have impacts on the detection system. To improve the performance of CDs sensor for Hg2+, we optimized the detection conditions before quantitative analysis of Hg2+ using the proposed method. At room temperature, the effect of the incubation time on the reaction system was shown in Fig. S2(a). It is clear that the quenched effect reached maximum when the reaction time was 30min and then sustained relatively stable. Hence, 30min was identified as the optimal incubation time. As revealed in Fig. S2(b), with the temperature increased from 10 to 60°C, the quenched effect of Hg2+ toward CDs kept stable, which suggested that temperature showed no effect on the detection character of CDs. To make sure obtain stable signal, the effect of pH on the reaction system was also tested. The result reveals that when the pH was changed from 3.0 to 9.0, the ration F 1/F 0 (F 0 and F 1 are fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively) increased firstly and then decreased, and the best quenched effect was achieved at 6–8 (Fig. S2(c)). At low pH, most of the functional groups are protonated and the competition of H+ with Hg(II) decreases the active sites. However, under alkaline conditions, the Hg2+ reacts with the OH− to form Hg(OH)2, which readily converts to HgO precipitate. Consequently, the quenched effect of the Hg2+ would be significantly weakened under acid or alkaline condition. So the 7.4 was chosen as the favorable pH due to pH 7.4 is similar to the physiological pH environment.

Besides selectivity, sensitivity is another important parameter to assess the performance of fluorescent CDs. To evaluate the sensitivity of the proposed method, fluorescence titration experiments were performed by adding of various concentrations of Hg2+ to CDs with a fixed concentration. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the as-prepared CDs initially exhibited a characteristic fluorescence emission band at 430nm with an excitation wavelength of 350nm in aqueous solution. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence was remarkably quenched, with the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreasing gradually with the increasing Hg2+ concentration due to the binding of Hg2+ onto the surface of CDs. The plot of the fluorescence intensity of CDs against the concentration of added Hg2+ is shown as an insert in Fig. 4(b). It is clear that the value of F 1/F 0 declined gradually with the enhancement of Hg2+ concentration, and reached a platform when a higher Hg2+ concentration (>15μM) was used. This phenomenon may be attributed to the hybridization between Hg2+ and the carboxylate or hydroxyl groups makes CDs close to each other (Scheme 1 ), which accelerates the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a substantial decrease of the fluorescence of CDs [51]. The inset reveals a good linear correlation between the value of (F 1/F 0)−1 and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 2–22μM, and the calibration curve can be expressed as (F 1/F 0)−1 =0.61324+0.1498c (c: μM) with the correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.99284. The detection limit for Hg2+ was obtained of 17nM calculated based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of eight blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve), which is comparable or even better than those reported parameters in the literature (shown in Table 1 ). The results demonstrated the reliability of CDs as an excellent candidate for the determination of sub-micromolar concentration Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the detection of l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that the thiol group in the molecular Cys can form a heavy metal-thiol bond, which is much stronger than the electrostatic interaction [47]. For CDs-Hg2+ system, l-Cys may cause a reversible Hg2+ desorption and lead to an “OFF–ON” fluorescence switching. Therefore, we explored the CDs-Hg2+ system to probe l-Cys based on the competitive binding mechanism among l-Cys, Hg2+ and CDs (Scheme 1). As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence date at 0min was corresponding to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Hg2+. However, the quenched fluorescence of the CDs-Hg2+ complex was recovered immediately within 1min upon addition of l-Cys, and then the fluorescence intensity remained stable. The explanation for the fluorescence enhancement process is: l-Cys have stronger binding preference toward Hg2+ than CDs due to the formation of Hg2+-SR bond, which disturbs the interaction between Hg2+ and CDs. Thus, Hg2+ was able to be easily separated from the surface of CDs due to the participation of l-Cys, then fluorescence of CDs restored. For further demonstrate above explanation, the zeta-potential measurements were carried out and the results were showed in Table 2 . Along with the addition of Hg2+, the negative charge density of CDs declined and a positive surface potential obtained. This result suggests that electrostatic attraction plays an important role on the conjugation of CDs with Hg2+. However, the addition of l-Cys changed the zeta-potential from positive to negative, which indicated that Hg2+ separated from the surface of CDs and CDs re-exposed. All above results indicated that the CDs-Hg2+ system able to sever as an effective fluorescence sensor for l-Cys detection.

It is reported that the thiol group in the molecular Cys can form a heavy metal-thiol bond, which is much stronger than the electrostatic interaction [47]. For CDs-Hg2+ system, l-Cys may cause a reversible Hg2+ desorption and lead to an “OFF–ON” fluorescence switching. Therefore, we explored the CDs-Hg2+ system to probe l-Cys based on the competitive binding mechanism among l-Cys, Hg2+ and CDs (Scheme 1). As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence date at 0min was corresponding to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Hg2+. However, the quenched fluorescence of the CDs-Hg2+ complex was recovered immediately within 1min upon addition of l-Cys, and then the fluorescence intensity remained stable. The explanation for the fluorescence enhancement process is: l-Cys have stronger binding preference toward Hg2+ than CDs due to the formation of Hg2+-SR bond, which disturbs the interaction between Hg2+ and CDs. Thus, Hg2+ was able to be easily separated from the surface of CDs due to the participation of l-Cys, then fluorescence of CDs restored. For further demonstrate above explanation, the zeta-potential measurements were carried out and the results were showed in Table 2. Along with the addition of Hg2+, the negative charge density of CDs declined and a positive surface potential obtained. This result suggests that electrostatic attraction plays an important role on the conjugation of CDs with Hg2+. However, the addition of l-Cys changed the zeta-potential from positive to negative, which indicated that Hg2+ separated from the surface of CDs and CDs re-exposed. All above results indicated that the CDs-Hg2+ system able to sever as an effective fluorescence sensor for l-Cys detection.

Besides, the UV–vis spectrum of this sensing system was studied to validate the detection mechanism (Fig. 5 ). The aqueous solution of CDs exhibited strong absorption peak around 340nm, but the absorption peak decreased significantly with the addition of Hg2+, which may be contributed to the inner filter effect [33]. However, with the participation of l-Cys, the peak belonged to CDs was restored obviously, indicating the release of CDs. These results were consistent with the observations of the fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the novel sensor for l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: At room temperature, the influences of pH and incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Hg2+-l-Cys system were studied. It can be seen from Fig. S4(a) that the best fluorescence recovery of CDs-Hg2+ system was achieved at 7.5–8.0. Thus, the following l-Cys detection was conducted when the pH of reaction system was 7.5. In addition, the effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence system was recorded for 60min every 5min. Fig. S4(b) reveals that the recovery effect reached maximum when the incubation time reached 30min, and then the fluorescence signal sustained stable. Hence, to obtain stable signal and save time, the fluorescence intensity was recorded after the system had reacted for 30min.

At room temperature, the influences of pH and incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Hg2+-l-Cys system were studied. It can be seen from Fig. S4(a) that the best fluorescence recovery of CDs-Hg2+ system was achieved at 7.5–8.0. Thus, the following l-Cys detection was conducted when the pH of reaction system was 7.5. In addition, the effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence system was recorded for 60min every 5min. Fig. S4(b) reveals that the recovery effect reached maximum when the incubation time reached 30min, and then the fluorescence signal sustained stable. Hence, to obtain stable signal and save time, the fluorescence intensity was recorded after the system had reacted for 30min.

Then, the fluorescence response of CDs-Hg2+ system used for l-Cys detection was investigated in the presence of increasing concentration of l-Cys. As displayed in Fig. 6(a), with the concentration of l-Cys increased, the fluorescence intensity of the system enhanced gradually. The plot of the fluorescence enhanced against the concentration of added l-Cys was shown in Fig. 6(b). It is clear that the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of l-Cys exhibits a linear response to the l-Cys concentration in the range of 2–20μM (R 2 =0.99329), and the calibration curve can be express as F 2/F 1 =2.90265+0.01928c (c: μM). Here, F 1 and F 2 are the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Hg2+ in the absence and presence of l-Cys, respectively. The detection limit is calculated to be 0.29μM based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of eight blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve). The repeatability of the sensing system was evaluated by operating eight repeated tests of 10μM l-Cys, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 0.32%, which suggested the reliability of the propose that CDs-Hg2+ system was used as sensor for l-Cys monitoring (Table S1).

Then, the fluorescence response of CDs-Hg2+ system used for l-Cys detection was investigated in the presence of increasing concentration of l-Cys. As displayed in Fig. 6(a), with the concentration of l-Cys increased, the fluorescence intensity of the system enhanced gradually. The plot of the fluorescence enhanced against the concentration of added l-Cys was shown in Fig. 6(b). It is clear that the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of l-Cys exhibits a linear response to the l-Cys concentration in the range of 2–20μM (R 2 =0.99329), and the calibration curve can be express as F 2/F 1 =2.90265+0.01928c (c: μM). Here, F 1 and F 2 are the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Hg2+ in the absence and presence of l-Cys, respectively. The detection limit is calculated to be 0.29μM based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of eight blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve). The repeatability of the sensing system was evaluated by operating eight repeated tests of 10μM l-Cys, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 0.32%, which suggested the reliability of the propose that CDs-Hg2+ system was used as sensor for l-Cys monitoring (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the CDs-Hg2+ system for l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensing system's specificity for l-Cys detection, the fluorescence analyses of the CDs-Hg2+ were carried out in the present of other amino acids including Alanine (Ala), Arginine (Arg), Aspartic acid (Asp), Histidine (His), Methionine (Met), Phenylalanine (Phe), Proline (Pro), Serine (Ser), Threonine (Thr), Tryptophan (Trp), Valine (Val), and Lysine (Lys). The fluorescence dates were collected from solutions containing different amino acid. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 7(a) that the presence of other amino acids showed no obvious interfering effects on the fluorescence of CDs-Hg2+ system, i.e. only the addition of thiol-containing l-Cys can result in the fluorescence enhancement. Fig. 7(b) shows the photos of CDs-Hg2+ aqueous solution with different amino acids (10μM) under UV light. These results demonstrate that the CDs-Hg2+ system shows a good specificity toward l-Cys. Furthermore, to investigate the anti-interference of the sensing system toward l-Cys, potential interfering effects from above amino acids were researched, and the results were shown in Fig. 6(c). The influence of these amino acids with no thiol groups is limited for l-Cys sensing, even in the presence of 10-fold amount. Thus, the CDs-Hg2+ can be explored as a highly selective sensing system for l-Cys with good anti-interference.

In view of the above-mentioned higher performance of the CDs, bioimaging applications of CDs for monitoring of Hg2+ in living cells were carried out. Importantly, the cytotoxicity effects play a key role in determining the applicability of CDs as fluorescence imaging agents and should be assessed before employing them for bioimaging. Thus, MTT assay was conducted [58] and the results of cell viability have been presented in Fig. 8(a). Both NIH-3T3 and HL-7702 cells did not show any decline in cell viability when the concentration of CDs was increased to 0.2mgmL−1. The survival rate of NIH-3T3 cells was about 85% at a high concentration of 0.5mgmL−1, while HL-7702 cells accounted for over 88% cell viability at that concentration. Overall, the CDs showed little cytotoxic effects in NIH-3T3 and HL-7702 cells and the results portrayed the biocompatibility of CDs for bioimaging. As a proof-of-concept, NIH-3T3 cells were incubated with CDs in culture medium for 24h at 37°C, and a bright fluorescence was observed from within the cells (Fig. 8(b)). CDs can be observed mainly in the cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area, especially around the cell nucleus. The cells were then supplemented with Hg2+ in the growth medium for another 30min, and the fluorescent intensity obviously decreases (Fig. 8(c)). A bright-field transmission image of cells treated with CDs and Hg2+ confirmed that the cells were viable throughout the imaging experiments (Fig. 8(a), (c), (e)). After above cells were incubated with l-Cys for another 30min, fluorescence image of the cells revealed a remarkable enhancement of intracellular fluorescence. These initial experiments on living cells demonstrated that CDs is cell-permeable and primarily little toxic to the cell culture. It is proven the great potential of CDs for fundamental biology research.

To test the practicality of the CDs in real water samples (Water samples were collected from different pools in Tianjin Polytechnic University), standard recovery experiments for the analysis of Hg2+ and l-Cys were performed. Standard addition solutions of different concentrations were added into the water sample and the final concentrations were adjusted to certain level. As listed in Table S2, the %Recoveries of Hg2+ were calculated as 93.1–95.8%, while that of l-Cys were estimated to be 101.2–105.8%. Further, the RSD ranges were both less than 5%, and thus, quite acceptable. These results indicated that analytical performance for the detection of Hg2+ and l-Cys in water samples was satisfactory.

To test the practicality of the CDs in real water samples (Water samples were collected from different pools in Tianjin Polytechnic University), standard recovery experiments for the analysis of Hg2+ and l-Cys were performed. Standard addition solutions of different concentrations were added into the water sample and the final concentrations were adjusted to certain level. As listed in Table S2, the %Recoveries of Hg2+ were calculated as 93.1–95.8%, while that of l-Cys were estimated to be 101.2–105.8%. Further, the RSD ranges were both less than 5%, and thus, quite acceptable. These results indicated that analytical performance for the detection of Hg2+ and l-Cys in water samples was satisfactory.

In conclusion, a simple and low toxicity dual-sensor was presented and used for the analyses of Hg2+ and l-Cys with the use of fluorescence switching of CDs in aqueous solution. The strategy presented can be easily accomplished with one-step rapid operation and exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ versus various other cations and l-Cys versus other potentially interfering amino acid, simultaneously. The Hg2+ induced aggregation of CDs accelerated the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through fluorescent charge transfer process, and led to significant fluorescence quenching. On the other hand, a significant fluorescence enhancement was observed when l-Cys was added into CDs-Hg2+ system. This occurred because of that l-Cys have stronger binding preference toward Hg2+ than CDs due to the formation of Hg2+-SR bond, displacing the weak electrostatic interactions. During the two processes, i.e. fluorescence “On–Off” and “On–Off”, good linearity for detecting Hg2+ was attained over the concentration range from 2 to 22μM with a detection limit of 0.017μM, while the concentrations of l-Cys ranging from 2 to 20μM exhibited a linear relationship under the optimized conditions. This CDs-based sensor has been successfully used for visual identification and quantitation of Hg2+ and l-Cys in real water samples with satisfactory results. Importantly, the CDs show low cytotoxicity and good cell-permeability, thus successfully applied for the fluorescence imaging of NIH-3T3 cells, demonstrating many broaden potential avenues in biosensing, bioimaging and other purposes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21174103, and 51303132) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.057.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is an associate professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). He received his Ph.D. degree from Tianjin University in 2007. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.

Dechao Shi received his B.Sc. in chemistry from Inner Mongolia Agricultural University (Hohhot, China). At present, he is a M.E. student in Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (Tianjin, China). His current research interest involves the design of fluorescent probes.

Tancheng Zheng is currently a graduate student in the Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (Tianjin, China), under the supervision of associate professor Fanyong Yan. His research interests include the design and synthesis of Rhodamine based sensor for metal ions.
Kaiyi Yun is an undergraduate student in the Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China).

Xuguang Zhou is a professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China), major in analytical chemistry. Her current research interests are focused on Nano and biomedical materials.

Li Chen received her Ph.D. degree in 1999 from Hokkaido University, Japan. She is a professor in the college of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). Her main research fields include: intelligent polymer, fluorescent chemosensors, biodegradable biopolymers, and nanotechnology.
